metadata.title: Weight Loss Calculator - Healthy Deficit & Timeline | FitMetrics
metadata.description: Calculate daily calories for healthy weight loss using TDEE minus controlled deficit, with goal-weight timeline and practical macro targets.

title: Weight Loss Calculator
fullTitle: Healthy Weight Loss - Calorie Deficit Planning
tagline: How many calories do you need to lose weight safely?
shortDesc: Set your daily calorie deficit, estimate timeline to goal weight, and get practical macros for sustainable fat loss.
importance: Unstructured dieting often leads to excessive restriction, poor adherence, and muscle-loss risk. A calibrated deficit with realistic pace supports safer, more sustainable fat loss.
description: This calculator estimates maintenance calories (TDEE) using Mifflin-St Jeor plus activity factor, then subtracts a controlled daily deficit based on your selected loss pace. It estimates timeline using the common population-level approximation that 1 kg body weight corresponds to about 7,700 kcal.
formula:
Step 1 - BMR (Mifflin-St Jeor):
  Male:   10xW + 6.25xH - 5xA + 5
  Female: 10xW + 6.25xH - 5xA - 161

Step 2 - TDEE = BMR x Activity Factor

Step 3 - Weight Loss Calories = TDEE - Deficit
  Gentle:     -250 kcal/day
  Moderate:   -500 kcal/day
  Aggressive: -750 kcal/day

Step 4 - Timeline estimate:
  Weeks ~= (CurrentWeight - GoalWeight) / [ (Deficit x 7) / 7700 ]
note: Timeline estimates are approximations. Real outcomes depend on adherence, activity changes, sleep, medications, stress, and adaptive changes in maintenance calories. WHO does not publish a single adult weight-loss equation; this uses open energy-balance methods.
source: Mifflin MD et al., Am J Clin Nutr (1990) · FAO/WHO/UNU Human Energy Requirements (2004) · WHO Obesity and Overweight Fact Sheet (2024)

healthRisks.title: Why Structured Weight Loss Matters
healthRisks.intro: Weight loss should reduce health risk while protecting lean mass and long-term adherence.

overweightItems.desc: Very low calories can increase fatigue, hunger, and rebound risk, and may raise lean-mass loss risk if protein and resistance training are inadequate.
Calorie restriction without adequate protein and strength training can reduce resting metabolic rate over time by increasing lean tissue loss.
Aggressive timelines often lead to poor adherence. A steady, moderate pace is usually more sustainable and easier to maintain after goal weight is reached.

healthRisks.underweightNote: For diabetes, thyroid disease, eating disorders, pregnancy, or chronic illness, use clinician-guided plans rather than generic deficit calculators.

limitations.title: How to Use This Calculator Most Effectively
limitations.intro: Use this as a high-quality starting estimate and personalize with trend-based feedback:

items.title: Start moderate, then adjust
items.desc: A -500 kcal/day deficit is a practical default for many adults. Adjust up or down every 2-4 weeks based on progress, hunger, and recovery.

items.title: Track weekly averages
items.desc: Daily body weight fluctuates from water, sodium, glycogen, and digestion. Use weekly average changes to judge whether your deficit is working.

items.title: Protect lean mass
items.desc: Keep protein adequate and continue resistance training during fat-loss phases. This improves body-composition quality and supports metabolic health.

faq.q: How many calories should I cut to lose weight?
faq.a: A common practical range is about 250-750 kcal/day below maintenance, depending on current body size, adherence capacity, and medical context.

faq.q: What is a safe weekly weight-loss pace?
faq.a: For many adults, moderate and consistent loss is preferable to aggressive crash dieting. Sustainable pace usually delivers better long-term maintenance.

faq.q: Why does my timeline keep changing?
faq.a: As body weight and activity change, maintenance calories also change. Recalculate every few weeks and update your target accordingly.

faq.q: Can I lose fat without losing muscle?
faq.a: You can reduce muscle-loss risk by using a controlled deficit, eating enough protein, and doing progressive resistance training.
No. This is an educational planning tool. If you have medical conditions or use weight-affecting medications, follow clinician guidance.
